does: This paper introduces a formal verification framework for large language model pipelines that certifies the deterministic structured computations around the model rather than the model weights themselves. Using Lean 4, the framework establishes trust boundaries by requiring certificates to pass kernel type-checking and audits against a minimal trusted axiom set (propext, Classical.choice, Quot.sound). The technical contributions include three certificate families—conflict-aware bilattice grounding with an emission-gate soundness lemma, embedding sensitivity and paraphrase stability, and Hoare-style agent action semantics—plus two operators: a Maximal Certifiable Residue that handles abstention by computing maximum-weight certifiable claims with audit logs, and a Compositional Stability theorem that derives closed-form perturbation budgets across pipeline layers from per-layer gains and margins.
connects: This work extends prior research in formal verification of machine learning systems and trust-boundary architectures by adapting Lean 4 proof-carrying code techniques to practical LLM deployment. It builds on foundational work in certified computation and auditable ML systems, where certificates provide machine-checkable evidence of correctness properties. The framework responds to growing concerns about verifying behavior in complex LLM pipelines that combine model inference with deterministic preprocessing, grounding, and action execution. It connects to research on formal semantics for autonomous agents, stability analysis in neural systems, and compositional reasoning about layered systems, translating abstract verification goals into concrete certificate families that practitioners can audit and maintain.
enables: This framework enables future development of trustworthy LLM system deployment by providing auditable, formally-verified pipelines suitable for safety-critical applications. The certificate architecture supports incremental adoption—practitioners can verify individual pipeline stages and compose them with formal guarantees. The approach opens directions for strengthening trust assumptions through additional certificate families targeting specific failure modes, for scaling formal verification to larger models through better abstention and residue handling, and for extending the framework to distributed and multi-agent LLM systems. It also enables research into automating certificate generation from pipeline specifications and combining these certificates with model-level guarantees, potentially creating hybrid verification schemes that reason about both deterministic structure and learned components.
